threats.title: VoteWeighting removeNominee Corrupts Global Weight Sum Slope
threats.hypothesis_description: When an admin removes a nominee via `removeNominee`, the nominee's bias is subtracted from the global `pointsSum`, but its slope is not subtracted from `pointsSum[nextTime].slope` or `changesSum`. This causes the global weight sum to decay faster than the actual sum of individual nominee weights, permanently breaking relative weight calculations for all other active nominees.
threats.investigation_rationale: This breaks the core mathematical invariant of the gauge controller. If the global slope is artificially steep, the total sum will reach zero prematurely or become corrupted, disrupting all cross-chain emission distributions.

proof_of_signal.pattern: pointsSum[nextTime].bias is updated to newSum without updating pointsSum[nextTime].slope

preconditions: Admin calls removeNominee on an active nominee with a non-zero slope

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Global slope remains artificially steep after nominee removal
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Breaks protocol emission distribution, causing DoS or unfair allocations to remaining nominees

economic_questions: How quickly does the global sum decay to zero after a removal?
Can an attacker exploit the corrupted relative weights before the system halts?
validation_requirements: Simulate a nominee removal with active votes and observe pointsSum decay over subsequent weeks
Verify if revokeRemovedNomineeVotingPower mitigates the issue or only fixes changesSum

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Protocol disruption (DoS) rather than direct profit

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Complete disruption of staking incentive distribution

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All active staking programs and their participants

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: pointsSum slope vs sum of individual nominee slopes

threats.title: Immediate State Update of Tokenomics Parameters Breaks Epoch Isolation
threats.hypothesis_description: Changes to `devsPerCapital`, `codePerDev`, and `epsilonRate` update state variables immediately, rather than being deferred to the next epoch like other parameters (`nextEpochLen`, `nextVeOLASThreshold`). This allows an admin to manipulate the current epoch's Inverse Discount Factor (IDF) and reward distributions retroactively before `checkpoint` is called.
threats.investigation_rationale: The protocol's design intent (stated in comments) is that parameter changes take effect in the next epoch to prevent mid-epoch manipulation. Immediate updates break this isolation, allowing retroactive changes to already-accumulated donations.

proof_of_signal.pattern: State variables updated immediately instead of using next* variables

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Admin observes donations, changes parameters to maximize/minimize IDF for the current epoch
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Admin can retroactively alter the split between treasury and component/agent rewards for already-received donations

economic_questions: How much can the IDF be manipulated mid-epoch?
What is the maximum financial impact on component/agent rewards?
validation_requirements: Simulate mid-epoch parameter changes and calculate the difference in finalized rewards

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Variable depending on total epoch donations

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Shifts value between Treasury and unit owners

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: IDF calculation sensitivity to devsPerCapital and codePerDev
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Compare intended deferred update vs actual immediate update

threats.title: effectiveBond Accounting Fails to Deduct Shortfalls During Year Changes
threats.hypothesis_description: During a year change where the inflation rate drops, the actual `maxBond` for the epoch can be less than the previously estimated `curMaxBond`. The `checkpoint` function only increases `effectiveBond` if actual > estimated, failing to deduct the shortfall and permanently inflating the protocol's bonding capacity.
threats.investigation_rationale: This breaks the conservation of OLAS inflation allocated for bonding. If `effectiveBond` is artificially inflated, the protocol may mint more OLAS for bonds than the inflation schedule actually permits.

proof_of_signal.pattern: if (incentives[4] > curMaxBond) { ... } without an else branch for shortfall

preconditions: New year's inflation rate is lower than the previous year's rate

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: No downward adjustment for effectiveBond when actual maxBond < estimated maxBond
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Year boundary naturally triggers the shortfall
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Protocol allows creation of bond programs exceeding actual inflation backing, diluting OLAS supply

economic_questions: What is the exact magnitude of the shortfall during each of the first 10 year boundaries?
Can this be weaponized by delaying checkpoints?
validation_requirements: Calculate inflationPerEpoch across a year boundary where inflation drops

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Thousands of unbacked OLAS minted via bonds per year boundary

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Unbacked token issuance dilutes existing holders

potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Difference between estimated and actual maxBond at boundaries

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Year 1 to Year 10 inflation schedule drops

threats.title: VoteWeighting Checkpoint Loop Fails to Advance Time When Exceeding MAX_NUM_WEEKS
threats.hypothesis_description: If `_getSum` or `_getWeight` is not called for more than `MAX_NUM_WEEKS` (53 weeks), the loop finishes without updating `timeSum` or `timeWeight`. Subsequent calls will re-process the exact same 53 weeks, permanently bricking the contract or specific nominee as time can never advance.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a critical liveness failure. While 53 weeks of inactivity is long, an attacker could intentionally avoid checkpointing a specific competing nominee to permanently brick it, removing it from emission distributions forever.

proof_of_signal.pattern: timeSum is only updated inside the loop if t > block.timestamp, leaving it unchanged if loop exhausts MAX_NUM_WEEKS

preconditions: A specific nominee or the global contract is not checkpointed for > 53 weeks

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: if (t > block.timestamp) { timeSum = t; } inside a bounded loop
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Attacker intentionally ignores a nominee for 53 weeks to brick it
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Attacker eliminates competing staking targets, securing more emissions for their own targets

economic_questions: Can an attacker reliably prevent others from checkpointing a target?
What happens to the global sum if one nominee is bricked?
validation_requirements: Simulate 54 weeks of inactivity and verify timeWeight does not advance
Verify subsequent calls revert or loop infinitely without progress

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Increased share of emissions for attacker's targets

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Permanent loss of functionality for affected targets

potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: All future emissions for the target

threats.title: Single Invalid Staking Target Causes Complete L1 Batch Claim Denial of Service
threats.hypothesis_description: On L1, `EthereumDepositProcessor._deposit` reverts the entire transaction if any target in the batch returns a zero emissions limit. A malicious deployer can disable their staking instance, causing any batch claim containing their instance to revert, blocking incentive distribution for all other valid targets.
threats.investigation_rationale: Unlike L2 dispensers which gracefully withhold amounts for invalid targets, the L1 processor hard-reverts. This allows a single malicious actor to DoS the Dispenser's batch claim functionality for L1 targets.
preconditions: Attacker deploys a staking instance on L1 and gets votes
Attacker disables the instance via StakingFactory.setInstanceStatus

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: Hard revert on zero emissions instead of withholding
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Attacker disables instance right before a batch claim
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Griefing attack preventing honest users from receiving their L1 staking incentives

economic_questions: Can users easily bypass this by claiming individually?
Does this block automated keeper bots from processing claims?
validation_requirements: Deploy malicious instance, disable it, and attempt a batch claim including it

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Delays incentive distribution on Ethereum L1

potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: All pending L1 staking incentives in the batch

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Batch size and target inclusion logic

threats.title: Wormhole Bridge Decimal Normalization Mismatch Causes Permanent Dust Loss
threats.hypothesis_description: Withheld amounts synced from L2 to L1 are not normalized on L1. When deducted from a normalized `transferAmount` during `claimStakingIncentives`, the resulting amount becomes un-normalized. When sent via the Wormhole bridge (which truncates to 8 decimals), the dust is permanently stuck in the `WormholeDepositProcessorL1` contract.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a systematic accounting leak. Because the Wormhole bridge silently truncates tokens to 8 decimals, any un-normalized dust resulting from the deduction of L2 withheld amounts will be permanently locked in the L1 deposit processor.
deployment_assumptions: Wormhole bridge is used for a target chain
threats.config_evidence: WormholeDepositProcessorL1.sol uses sendTokenWithPayloadToEvm which truncates to 8 decimals

proof_of_signal.pattern: transferAmount -= withheldAmount where withheldAmount is not normalized

preconditions: A staking target on L2 has a limitAmount with dust (non-zero in last 10 decimals)
The withheld amount is synced to L1 and deducted from a future transfer

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Deploying a staking contract with a dusty rewardsPerSecond to force dusty withheld amounts
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Systematic permanent locking of OLAS dust in the L1 deposit processor

economic_questions: How much dust can be locked per transaction?
Does the locked dust eventually cause insolvency in the Dispenser?
validation_requirements: Simulate a dusty withheld amount sync and subsequent claim via Wormhole
Check the balance of WormholeDepositProcessorL1 after the transfer

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Up to 10^10 wei per transaction locked

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Slow deflation of circulating OLAS due to permanent lock

potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Cumulative dust over thousands of transactions

threats.title: Queued Staking Deposits Bypass Re-verification of Target Validity
threats.hypothesis_description: The `redeem` function in L2 target dispensers does not re-verify the staking target's validity or emissions limit. A target that was valid when queued but later disabled can still receive the full queued amount, bypassing protocol emission limits.
threats.investigation_rationale: Queuing separates the verification step from the actual deposit. If the target's status changes during the queued period, the protocol incorrectly honors the outdated verification, distributing emissions to an invalid target.

proof_of_signal.pattern: redeem function deposits without calling verifyInstanceAndGetEmissionsAmount

preconditions: L2 dispenser has insufficient OLAS, causing a valid target's deposit to be queued
The target is subsequently disabled or its emissions limit is reduced
OLAS is supplied to the dispenser and redeem is called

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Attacker queues a large deposit, disables the target to avoid scrutiny, then redeems later
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Attacker extracts OLAS emissions into a disabled/invalid staking contract

economic_questions: Can an attacker intentionally drain the L2 dispenser to force queuing?
What is the maximum amount that can be queued and wrongly redeemed?
validation_requirements: Queue a deposit, disable the target via StakingFactory, and successfully redeem

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Sufficient to drain L2 dispenser temporarily

market_conditions_analysis.liquidity_requirements: Requires L2 dispenser to be temporarily underfunded
market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: When L2 dispenser balance is low

potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: All queued amounts for targets that become invalid

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Queue hash generation and redemption logic
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Verify absence of emissions check in redeem

threats.title: Service Donation Truncation Causes Permanent ETH Lock in Tokenomics
threats.hypothesis_description: When splitting donated ETH across service units, `amounts[i] / numServiceUnits` truncates the remainder. This remainder is included in `totalDonationsETH` (which dictates the Treasury's share) but is never credited to `pendingRelativeReward`. The truncated dust is permanently locked in the Treasury contract.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a systematic accounting leak. Because the Treasury allocates the full component/agent fraction based on `totalDonationsETH`, but the units only claim the truncated amounts, the difference is never claimed and remains locked forever.

proof_of_signal.pattern: uint96 amount = uint96(amounts[i] / numServiceUnits); truncates remainder

preconditions: A service receives a donation amount not perfectly divisible by its number of units

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: Sum of pendingRelativeReward < totalDonationsETH due to truncation
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Attacker creates services with many units to maximize truncation loss for donators
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Systematic permanent locking of ETH in the Treasury contract

economic_questions: How much ETH is lost per donation on average?
Can this be weaponized to drain donator funds without rewarding units?
validation_requirements: Simulate a donation of 100 wei to a service with 3 units
Verify 1 wei is permanently locked in the Treasury after all claims

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Up to (numServiceUnits - 1) wei per donation locked

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Slow accumulation of locked ETH in Treasury

potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Loss of fractional wei per donation
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Cumulative dust over thousands of donations

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Compare sum of claims vs total allocated
